stories.headline: Bomb Threat Forces Deplaning of United Airlines Flight at Denver International Airport
stories.summary:
A bomb threat Sunday evening forced approximately 200 passengers and seven crew members to deplane from United Airlines Flight 2408 at Denver International Airport before it departed. Passengers exited the aircraft via airstairs, and the plane was screened and cleared by airport staff and the Denver Police Department. No dangerous materials were found on the aircraft.

The flight, scheduled to depart for Washington Dulles International Airport at 5:58 p.m., ultimately left nearly six hours late at 11:33 p.m. Denver law enforcement is working with the FBI to investigate the source of the threat, which could constitute a federal crime involving threats to critical infrastructure.

FBI Denver Public Affairs Officer Vikki Migoya confirmed the joint investigation, noting that any threat or hoax threat against airport operations may be prosecuted federally. No arrests had been announced as of Monday.

stories.headline: Five Suspected IEDs Found and Removed from Western Boulder County
stories.summary:
Boulder County sheriff's deputies responded Sunday morning to a report of suspected improvised explosive devices found in a metal case near Ponderosa Way in western Boulder County, north of Pinecliffe. A person who opened the case discovered five suspected bombs and reported them to authorities. Investigators said the devices had features consistent with functioning IEDs, though whether they were functional had not yet been determined.

Deputies issued a shelter-in-place order around noon Sunday, which remained in effect until approximately 7 p.m. when the devices were contained and removed to a secure location. Bomb Squad technicians adjusted their tactics during the operation for safety reasons, extending the time needed to secure the devices.

Officials plan to dispose of the devices before conducting a final determination of their functionality. The Boulder County Sheriff's Office urged residents who encounter suspicious packages or items to leave the area immediately and call 911.

stories.headline: Denver Man Set to Have Murder Conviction Vacated After 27 Years; DA Agrees Conviction Was Wrongful
stories.summary:
Stephen Martinez, 58, who has served more than 27 years of a life sentence for the 1998 murder of a 4-month-old infant, is scheduled to appear in Denver District Court on Tuesday to have his conviction vacated. Both his attorneys with the Korey Wise Innocence Project at the University of Colorado Law School and the Denver District Attorney's Office agree the conviction should be overturned.

New evidence indicates the infant, Heather Mares, died of a heart attack caused by severe respiratory illness rather than from being shaken. Martinez's original attorneys failed to present evidence of the child's ongoing respiratory illness at the 2000 trial, leaving jurors without alternative explanations for her death. Prosecutors with DA John Walsh's office acknowledged in a court filing that there is a reasonable probability the trial outcome would have been different had that evidence been presented.

Martinez was also coerced into a false confession, according to his attorneys. A relative of Heather Mares told the Denver Post the family believes Martinez's conviction should stand, saying it felt like justice had forgotten the baby. The case is part of a broader national trend in which convictions based on shaken baby syndrome have faced increased scientific scrutiny.

stories.headline: PNC Bank to Lay Off 777 Employees at Lakewood Facility Following FirstBank Merger
stories.summary:
PNC Bank has filed a WARN Act notice with the state of Colorado indicating it will lay off 777 employees at the West Colfax Building in Lakewood, with separations expected to begin June 30, 2026. The layoffs come three months after PNC Financial Services Group finalized its $4.1 billion acquisition of Colorado's FirstBank, which was headquartered at the same Lakewood address for more than 60 years.

PNC said the layoffs result from consolidating overlapping roles across the two organizations, and noted that the West Colfax Building will remain open with other employees continuing to work there. The bank said it is retaining all client-facing roles and has designated Denver as a strategic technology hub for the enterprise, citing a strong local technology talent base.

PNC employs approximately 4,326 people in Colorado and operates 120 locations in the state following the merger. Lakewood's Economic Development Office said it is coordinating with state and county partners to deploy rapid response services for affected employees.

stories.headline: Colorado New Business Filings Hit Record High in Q1 2026, Despite Economic Uncertainty
stories.summary:
Nearly 55,000 new businesses registered in Colorado during the first quarter of 2026, a 12.3% increase year-over-year and the highest level of new filings recorded since the University of Colorado Boulder began tracking state data in 2005. Business renewals also rose 16.2% compared to the same period last year, according to the CU Boulder report released Monday.

CU Boulder economist Brian Lewandowski called the surge in filings "extraordinary," though he noted the causes are difficult to pinpoint since registration data does not include reasons for starting a business. He suggested recent activity may be partly driven by people seeking additional sources of income amid economic uncertainty, a trend observed in prior years.

Delinquencies — businesses late on their state registrations — rose 9.4%, but dissolutions fell 10.5%, and the two metrics have not been trending in tandem, suggesting late filers are resolving their status rather than closing. Entities in good standing rose 3.7% year-over-year. Colorado Secretary of State Jena Griswold said the state must continue to prioritize affordability despite the strong filing numbers.

stories.headline: Colorado Legislature Advances Elder Fraud Bill Allowing Banks to Delay Suspicious Transactions
stories.summary:
The Colorado Senate unanimously passed House Bill 1110 on Monday, a bipartisan measure that would give banks the authority to delay certain suspicious financial transactions made by customers aged 70 and older. The bill also requires bank employees who handle transactions or approve loans to report potential financial exploitation to law enforcement or county agencies. It now heads to Gov. Jared Polis, whose office said he will review the final version.

The legislation was prompted in part by the experience of Debbie Fox, an Arvada retiree who lost $58,000 in a romance scam in 2023 after being manipulated over several weeks by a fraudster who built emotional trust before requesting loans. Fox has since become an advocate for stronger protections and testified that a transaction delay would have allowed her to recognize the manipulation before the money was gone.

Colorado ranks eighth nationally in elder fraud complaints, with 2,905 filed in 2023 according to the FBI, which reported $3.4 billion stolen from older Americans that year. Sponsor Sen. Jessie Danielson, a Wheat Ridge Democrat, said financial abuse of the elderly is prevalent and the delay mechanism is a straightforward intervention. AARP advocates testified that delay is the single most effective tool against such scams.

stories.headline: Colorado Senate Advances Bill to Address Prison Overcrowding with Working Group, Earned-Time Changes
stories.summary:
The Colorado Senate gave preliminary approval Friday to Senate Bill 159, a measure aimed at addressing the state's prison capacity crisis. The bill, sponsored by Democratic Sens. Julie Gonzales of Denver and Mike Weissman of Aurora, would create a working group to develop a corrections capacity management plan and make changes to earned-time rules for inmates.

The Department of Corrections was authorized in the 2026-27 budget to add 941 beds for male inmates, but locations remain undecided after the Joint Budget Committee rejected Gov. Jared Polis's request for two new prisons. The JBC instead approved a budget placeholder allowing the department to contract for beds at two previously closed private prisons in Trinidad and Burlington.

Approximately 4,600 inmates are past their mandatory parole dates but remain incarcerated due to a shortage of parole officers and community corrections approvals, according to policymakers and advocates. The working group's final report is due June 30, 2028, with an interim report due in December. Critics have attributed the capacity crisis to what they call a Democrat-led closure of prisons that prioritized offenders over public safety.

stories.headline: Colorado Public Utilities Commission Overrules Elbert County, Clears Path for Xcel Transmission Line
stories.summary:
The Colorado Public Utilities Commission ruled last week that statewide interest in reliable and economical electric service outweighs Elbert County's local land-use authority, overturning the county's 2025 denial of permits for a portion of Xcel Energy's Colorado Power Pathway transmission project. The $1.7 billion project involves approximately 48 miles of new lines through Elbert County as part of a 550-mile, 12-county infrastructure expansion.

The Elbert County Board of Commissioners had unanimously rejected the permits, citing an incomplete application and arguing Xcel should route the line farther east to reduce impacts on landowners and wildlife. Xcel said it evaluated 500 miles of potential routes before selecting the current alignment, and subsequently sued the county and asked the PUC to intervene.

PUC Chairman Eric Blank acknowledged the decision placed the commission in a difficult position, noting substantial room for improvement in Xcel's outreach to landowners, while concluding the record clearly supported the statewide need for the line. The PUC is expected to issue a similar ruling on El Paso County's permit denial by April 29. Elbert County may petition the PUC for reconsideration.

stories.headline: Colorado Department of Human Services Under Investigation Amid Toxic Workplace Allegations, $2.8 Million in Settlements
stories.summary:
The Colorado Department of Human Services contracted with Denver-based Flynn Investigations Group in January to probe workplace conditions within the agency, following a cascade of formal complaints, high leadership turnover, and nearly $2.8 million paid to departing employees in settlement agreements since 2019. Seven current and former employees described a toxic work environment marked by abusive, inappropriate, and demeaning leadership that damaged staff mental health.

The settlements have addressed alleged discrimination based on pay, age, gender, and disability, as well as whistleblower protection violations and retaliatory firings, among other claims. The $25,000 investigation remains ongoing, and department officials declined an interview request and did not respond to a list of questions from the Denver Post.

The Colorado Department of Human Services is the state's second-largest agency, with more than 4,800 employees responsible for child welfare, services to disabled and older adults, and 12 youth detention facilities. A department spokesperson said it strives to maintain a respectful workplace and takes immediate action when investigations find policy or legal violations.

stories.headline: Xcel Energy Considers Preemptive Power Shutoffs in Parts of Colorado Wednesday Amid Peak Wildfire Danger
stories.summary:
Xcel Energy announced Monday it is planning for a possible Public Safety Power Shutoff beginning Wednesday morning for customers in Garfield, Mesa, and Pitkin counties, as well as parts of the San Luis Valley including Alamosa, Conejos, Costilla, Rio Grande, and Saguache counties. The utility cited a combination of strong winds, low humidity, and unseasonably warm and dry conditions forecast to create critical fire danger across Colorado's Eastern Plains, Front Range foothills, and high mountain valleys.

The National Weather Service has issued a red flag warning for the San Luis Valley, and the region is experiencing extreme to exceptional drought conditions. Xcel said weather conditions are expected to begin improving around 7 p.m. Wednesday, at which point crews would begin restoring power. The utility did not specify how many customers could be affected.

In areas not subject to the preemptive shutoffs, Xcel may implement Enhanced Powerline Safety Settings, which make lines more sensitive to faults but can extend the duration of outages because crews must inspect lines before restoring service. Strong winds could also cause additional unplanned outages by damaging power infrastructure.

stories.headline: Denver Water to Drain Popular Antero Reservoir This Summer in Response to Record Drought
stories.summary:
Denver Water announced Monday it will drain Antero Reservoir in Park County this summer, moving roughly 20,000 acre-feet of water downstream to Cheesman Reservoir to prevent an estimated 5,000 acre-feet from evaporating. The move is a direct response to what the utility describes as a record-setting drought affecting its system, which serves 1.5 million people across the Denver metro area.

Antero Reservoir, located on the Middle Fork of the South Platte River south of Fairplay, has the highest evaporative rate of Denver Water's reservoirs and was 88% full on Monday. The drainage will close the reservoir to recreation, including camping, through the end of the year. Denver Water will work with Colorado Parks and Wildlife to minimize fish deaths during the process.

The reservoir was last drained in response to drought in 2002. Denver Water officials said the decision on when to refill Antero will depend on how drought conditions evolve. An acre-foot of water is roughly equivalent to the annual water use of three to four households.

stories.headline: Wildfire Tax Relief Expiration Leaves Colorado Marshall Fire Survivors Facing Potential Tax Hit on Settlements
stories.summary:
A federal tax exemption on wildfire-related settlement payments expired at the end of 2025, leaving survivors of Colorado's 2021 Marshall Fire and other recent U.S. wildfires potentially liable for income taxes on compensation received from utilities blamed for igniting the blazes. A bipartisan House bill to restore the tax relief passed out of committee last month, but its timeline for a floor vote and Senate action remain uncertain.

Jenn Kaaoush, a Marshall Fire survivor and town council member in Superior, Colorado, said affected residents have low expectations that Congress will act in time. Without relief, survivors could face tax rates as high as 37% on large settlements intended to help them rebuild homes that were often underinsured against rapidly rising construction costs.

Congress has previously extended disaster tax relief for wildfire settlements but has allowed gaps between the laws, leaving some survivors in financial limbo at a critical stage of rebuilding. Colorado, Hawaii, California, and Oregon survivors are all affected by the expired exemption. Advocates say the tax burden could determine whether entire communities are rebuilt.

weather.notable: About 21 degrees above the average high for this time of year!

joke.joke: My wife and I went to a marriage retreat. They said to talk about your feelings. I said, 'I feel like this retreat is too long.'

topStory.headline: Virginia Voters Decide Redistricting Referendum With House Control in the Balance
topStory.summary:
Virginia voters are casting ballots Tuesday on a constitutional amendment that would temporarily grant the Democratic-controlled General Assembly the power to redraw the state's congressional district boundaries. If approved, the measure — already passed by the legislature in February and signed by Democratic Gov. Abigail Spanberger — would reshape Virginia's 11 congressional districts in a way that could give Democrats the advantage in as many as 10 of them, a net gain of four seats from the current six-to-five Democratic split.

Polling indicates an extremely tight outcome, with most surveys showing the 'yes' side holding a slim edge near or within the margin of error. An April 15 poll by Quantus Insights showed approximately 51 percent of likely voters in favor of the amendment versus 47 percent opposed, with a margin of error of about three percentage points. President Trump held a tele-rally the night before the vote, urging Republicans to the polls and calling the referendum a 'shameful' attempt to 'disenfranchise' Republican voters, while former President Barack Obama cut a television advertisement supporting the measure, arguing it would 'level the playing field.'

The referendum is the latest development in a mid-decade redistricting conflict that escalated in July 2025, when Texas Republican lawmakers, at Trump's urging, redrew their congressional map to benefit Republicans — an unusual move outside the traditional post-Census redistricting cycle. Trump subsequently pressed Republican-led legislatures in other states, including Missouri, to make similar changes. Democrats in states such as California responded with their own remapping efforts, with California voters approving altered maps by a more than two-to-one margin.

Opponents of the Virginia measure, including former Republican governors Glenn Youngkin and George Allen, have pointed to Princeton University's Gerrymandering Project grading Virginia's existing maps as among the fairest in the country, hoping that distinction persuades independent voters to reject the change. Obama's prior public statements against partisan gerrymandering have been used in 'no' campaign advertisements, though Obama disputed that framing in his recent endorsement spot. Groups backing the referendum have significantly outraised those opposing it, according to the nonpartisan Virginia Public Access Project.

The stakes extend well beyond Virginia's borders. The U.S. House is currently divided 217-to-213 in favor of Republicans, meaning a shift of just a few seats could determine which party controls the chamber for the final two years of Trump's term following the 2026 midterm elections. Under the proposed amendment, the General Assembly's redistricting authority would expire in October 2030, when power would revert to the state's nonpartisan redistricting commission. New district boundaries, if the referendum passes, would be in place in time for the November midterms.
